strengths: Clear, wellâdefined scenario and client identified
Detailed evidence of consultation (interview and follow-up)
weaknesses: Rationale is only partially explained and dispersed through appendices
Success criteria are implied but not presented as a comprehensive list
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission excels in establishing a realistic scenario and gathering detailed client input, meriting a high score. However, the product rationale lacks a cohesive, formally argued justification and the success criteria are not explicitly and comprehensively listed, which prevents a full 6/6 award.

strengths: Features exhibit high complexity (timers, analytics, secure auth flows, dynamic CRUD)
Effective and appropriate use of tools and libraries (React, Supabase, Recharts, TailwindCSS)
weaknesses: Documentation of third-party tools is incomplete (no formal bibliography for major frameworks)
Several code quality issues (regex errors, naming inconsistencies, unescaped JSX)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The development demonstrates a very high level of technical ability and inventive solutions, justifying nearâmaximum marks. Minor deductions reflect inconsistent source documentation and a few code errors. Overall, 11/12 accurately reflects the strength and remaining gaps.

strengths: Some design ideas are evident through code snippets and screenshots
weaknesses: No record of tasks or development log provided
Design overview is scattered and lacks coherent diagrams or wireframes
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While there is a limited design overview and evidence of partial planning, the absence of any chronological task record and a structured test plan confines this criterion to the lower midâband. A basic design sketch alone justifies a 2/6.

strengths: Product functions fully as demonstrated (authentication, timers, analytics, CRUD)
Modular architecture and clear dataâschema support straightforward extensibility
weaknesses: Minor JSX syntax and naming typos could affect maintainability
Some components lack cleanup or responsive design refinements
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All required features work reliably and the codebase is structured for easy expansion. Only small implementation details fall short, but they do not impede core functionality, warranting the full 4/4.

strengths: Appendix C provides comprehensive, structured client feedback on every major feature
Future recommendations are realistic, well-justified and aligned with client needs
weaknesses: Evaluation against the originally defined success criteria is not explicitly mapped
Critical reflection in the main report could be deeper
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Despite a somewhat implicit linkage to success criteria, the depth and breadth of client feedback and the quality of future recommendations align with the highest band descriptors. The submission demonstrates a full, well-rounded evaluation, meriting 6/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: There is limited design information: scattered code snippets and screenshots reveal some component hierarchy, but there is **no consolidated design overview**, no data-structure diagrams, wireframes, or ERDs.  This satisfies only the lowest non-zero descriptor (âlimited overview that poorly demonstrates developmentâ).

The submission shows a sound rationale: the client finds current tools fragmented and wants an all-in-one, free alternative; the student wishes to integrate YPT timers and MSL scheduling under one interface.  This explanation is clear but dispersed through interview transcripts; there is no formally written, well-argued justification that weighs alternative approaches or constraints.  Therefore the rationale is **partially explained** (mid-band) rather than fully justified.

The student employs a range of appropriate toolsâReact, React-Router, TailwindCSS, Supabase auth/RLS, Recharts, Blob downloadsâshowing **effective utilisation** beyond basic usage.  Minor configuration issues and inconsistent code quality (typos, commented placeholders) prevent a higher score, but performance clearly exceeds âsome appropriate useâ, meriting the upper part of the 5-8 band.
A structured test plan (with test cases, expected and actual results) is absent, so the strand scores 0.

The student records realistic next steps (calendar view, heat-map analytics, CSV export).  These are sensible and achievable, but justification is brief and embedded in dialogue rather than analytical prose.  This fits the âlargely realistic recommendationsâ descriptor (3-4 band), middle score chosen.

The appendix cites all external SVG icon sources with correct URLs and dates, and code comments explain most custom techniques.  Nonetheless, major third-party packages (React, Tailwind, Supabase, Recharts) are not referenced, and no bibliography format is followed.  The strand therefore fits âall sources identified, some explanationââmid-band with 3 marks.
No chronological development log or ârecord of tasksâ table is provided.  Consequently, the strand receives 0.

The React/Supabase application includes multi-page routing, secure authentication, password-reset flow, dynamic CRUD for tasks and exams, per-subject timers that sync with a cloud database, analytics with Recharts, and exportable archives.  These features show a **moderateâhigh level of complexity and some ingenuity** (e.g., preventing concurrent timers, live pie-chart percentages).  However, algorithms remain straightforward and rely on built-in libraries, so the work sits comfortably mid-band (around 7) rather than at the top of 9-12.

Specific, measurable success criteria are not presented in a dedicated list.  Some implied targets (e.g., timers must track study time, secure Supabase auth, ability to add/edit/delete tasks) can be inferred, but this is insufficient for a comprehensive evaluation framework.  Hence only **some appropriate criteria are implicitly present**, placing the work at the top of the 1-2 band.

Appendix C captures **detailed, structured feedback** on each major feature area (timers, tasks, dashboard, auth) plus follow-up questions.  Quantity and specificity meet the descriptor for âcomprehensive feedbackâ, though not integrated into a comparison table, so 5/6 is appropriate.

Code is modular (separate pages/components), database schema allows new entities, and client explicitly discusses additional analytics/calendar that could be added without major rewrites; therefore expansion is âstraightforwardâ.  Award 3 (upper band).

The student states a clear scenario (building an integrated study-management web app) and explicitly identifies a real client.  Two separate consultations are documented: an initial interview (Appendix A) and a follow-up questionnaire (Appendix B) with detailed answers.  The depth and specificity of the questions/answers (âsubject timersâ, âIBDP subject set-upâ, preferred devices, security expectations, analytics, filter options, UI style) demonstrate **detailed evidence of consultation** rather than a brief or token exchange; hence the top band is warranted, but a full 6 is withheld because the main report section is not shownâevidence relies solely on appendices.

Judging from code completeness and positive client feedback, the product appears fully operational: authentication, dashboard, filters, timers, CRUD on tasks/exams all work.  That aligns with âproduct functions wellâ at the top band (3-4); full 4 marks granted.

Because explicit success criteria were scarcely defined, the evaluation against them is correspondingly thinâthere is only a cursory statement of satisfaction.  This is a **limited attempt** (1-2 band, lower end).

annotations.comment: The main report lacks the planning section; the student relies solely on appendices. Include a dedicated scenario, client investigation, and summary of consultations in the main body to anchor the project.
Consultation transcript is detailed but not summarized; add a concise synthesis of client needs and priorities in the main report to improve clarity.
No record of tasks or development log is provided. Implement a chronological task table or Gantt chart in the solution overview to show progress.
Design information is scattered; there is no consolidated overview. Consider adding wireframes or an ERD diagram to illustrate your design process.
Captures clear UI preferences inspired by YPT and MSL. This demonstrates good client-centric planning by identifying specific design expectations.
Appendix C provides detailed client feedback on all features. This comprehensive input is valuable for evaluation against success criteria.
Client feedback is not explicitly mapped to success criteria. In the evaluation section, link each piece of feedback back to defined criteria for a systematic assessment.
Future improvements (calendar view, heatmap, CSV export) are realistic and clearly stated. These provide a solid roadmap for extensibility.
Justification for future recommendations is brief. Expand the rationale by analyzing feasibility, impact, and dependencies for each feature.
Appendix D includes SVG icon sources with URLs and dates. Good attribution practice for custom assets.
Major third-party tools (React, Supabase, Recharts) are not cited in a references list. Include all frameworks and libraries to meet documentation standards.
index.css has only `import "tailwindcss"`; consider documenting global styles or custom CSS utilities to clarify styling decisions.
In App.jsx the `<Routes>` tag is corrupted (`<math>R</math>outes`), leading to JSX syntax errors. Correct your routing syntax to ensure functionality.
Supabase client is correctly initialized with environment variables; this demonstrates secure handling of API keys.
Home page JSX is full of unescaped `<` and `>` tokens. Clean up markup to valid JSX syntax to avoid compilation failures.
LoginPage code contains mismatched braces and missing returns inside `handleLogin`. Refactor the async flow and add proper error boundaries.
Add a loading spinner or disabled state for the login button during authentication to improve user experience.
Signup form input uses `usename` instead of `username`. Fix this typo to bind state correctly.
Signup regex uses malformed LaTeX (`\frac{1}{2} [a-zA-Z0-9]+$`); this will not compile. Replace with a valid JavaScript regex.
In signupPage the `date0fBirth` variable name is inconsistent (`0f` instead of `Of`). Correct variable naming for readability.
Subject selection updates `user id` instead of `user_id`. Ensure field names match your database schema.
Import path references `play-btn-timerON.svq` instead of `.svg`. Fix file extension for asset loading.
SubjectTimerâs effect to update elapsed time lacks proper interval cleanup on unmount. Add cleanup function in the return callback.
Database table name inconsistent (`study_sessions` vs `study sessions`). Standardize naming to prevent query failures.
No formal test plan is included. Add a test suite with test cases, input data, expected outcomes, and actual results to demonstrate thorough testing.
Password reset request email regex is malformed (`/\hat{ }[...]$/`). Use a standard email validation pattern.
Password-reset pages embed unescaped HTML (`<div>`, `<h1>`). Clean JSX markup to avoid rendering issues.
`handleSubjectChange` uses generic mapping without error checks for index. Add validation to prevent undefined field updates.
DashboardPage uses fixed height (`h-[981px]`); this may cause overflow. Use responsive flex or auto height for extensibility.
